title: Steel per m² Slab Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Steel per m² of Slab
description: Calculate steel reinforcement per m² of slab by type. Free calculator for all slab types. Get exact requirements.
intro: The Steel per m² Slab Calculator estimates how many kilograms of reinforcing steel (rebar) are needed per square meter of concrete slab, based on slab type. Steel reinforcement is critical for tensile strength in concrete structures — concrete handles compression well but fails in tension without rebar. The standard design formula starts with the reinforcement ratio ρ = As / (b × d), where As is the steel area, b is the slab width, and d is the effective depth. Typical values range from 10–15 kg/m² for simple solid slabs to 25–40 kg/m² for post-tensioned or waffle slabs. Engineers and contractors use this metric to budget materials, verify structural compliance with ACI 318, and cross-check shop drawings before construction begins.
keyTakeaway: **Steel weight (kg/m²) = ρ × d × 7,850 kg/m³**, where ρ is the reinforcement ratio (typically 0.0018–0.005 for slabs per ACI 318-19) and d is the effective slab depth in meters. For a 200 mm solid slab: 0.0025 × 0.165 m × 7,850 ≈ **3.24 kg/m² per mat** — two mats give ≈ 6.5 kg/m², plus chairs and ties ≈ 8–10 kg/m² total.
useCases: Estimating the rebar budget for a residential two-way solid slab (150–200 mm thick) before submitting a bid or purchase order to a steel supplier.
Verifying that a contractor's steel takeoff matches ACI 318-19 minimum reinforcement ratios for temperature and shrinkage control (ρ ≥ 0.0018 for Grade 60 bars).
Comparing material costs between a traditional solid slab and a ribbed or waffle slab during the schematic design phase of a multi-story office building.
Conducting a quick sanity check on structural engineer drawings before a construction loan appraisal or building permit submission to confirm steel quantities are within industry norms.
explanation:
## How It Is Calculated

The steel quantity per square meter of slab is derived from the **reinforcement ratio** combined with slab geometry and steel density:

```
Steel (kg/m²) = ρ × d × ρ_steel × n_mats + allowance

Where:
  ρ         = reinforcement ratio (As / b·d) — dimensionless
  d         = effective depth of slab (m) = total thickness − cover − bar_radius
  ρ_steel   = density of steel = 7,850 kg/m³
  n_mats    = number of reinforcement layers (1 or 2)
  allowance = ~10–15% added for laps, chairs, ties, and waste
```

**ACI 318-19 minimum ratios for slabs:**
- Temperature & shrinkage (Grade 60 / fy = 420 MPa): **ρ_min = 0.0018**
- Structural two-way slabs: **ρ ≈ 0.0025 – 0.005** depending on moment demand

A simpler practical formula used on site:

```
Steel (kg/m²) ≈ (bar_diameter² / 162) × (1000 / spacing_mm) × unit_weight_factor

Example: #4 bar (12.7 mm) @ 150 mm c/c
= (12.7² / 162) × (1000/150) = 0.994 kg/m per layer
Two layers + 12% waste ≈ 2.23 kg/m² (bottom mat only — add top mat similarly)
```

---

## Reference Table

| Slab Type | Thickness (mm) | Steel (kg/m²) | ρ Typical | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Traditional solid slab (one-way) | 120–180 | 8–15 | 0.0018–0.003 | Residential floors |
| Traditional solid slab (two-way) | 150–250 | 10–20 | 0.0025–0.005 | Office/commercial |
| Ribbed slab (joist slab) | 300–400 total | 12–18 | — | Ribs + topping |
| Waffle slab | 350–500 total | 20–30 | — | Long spans >8 m |
| Post-tensioned flat plate | 180–250 | 4–8 (passive) | — | PT strands separate |
| Flat slab (no beams) | 200–300 | 15–25 | 0.003–0.006 | Punching shear governs |
| Precast hollow-core | 150–320 | 3–6 (topping) | — | Prestressed strands in unit |
| Ground-bearing slab-on-grade | 100–150 | 5–10 | 0.0018 min | ACI 360R guidance |

*Values represent total steel including top bars, bottom bars, chairs, laps (10%) and tie wire (~1%). Source: ACI 318-19, PCI Design Handbook 8th Ed.*

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Residential Solid One-Way Slab (5 m span)
- **Slab:** 160 mm thick, 25 mm cover, #4 bars (12.7 mm ∅) @ 180 mm c/c bottom; #3 @ 300 mm c/c top (temperature steel)
- **Bottom steel:** (12.7²/162) × (1000/180) = 0.994 × 5.56 = 5.52 kg/m²
- **Top steel (temp.):** (9.5²/162) × (1000/300) = 0.557 × 3.33 = 1.85 kg/m²
- **Subtotal:** 7.37 kg/m² × 1.12 (laps + waste) = **8.3 kg/m²** ✔ (within 8–15 range)

### Case 2 — Commercial Two-Way Flat Slab (7.5 m × 7.5 m panel)
- **Slab:** 250 mm thick, 25 mm cover, #5 bars (15.9 mm ∅) @ 150 mm c/c both ways, top and bottom
- **Each layer:** (15.9²/162) × (1000/150) = 1.561 × 6.67 = 10.41 kg/m²
- **4 layers total:** 10.41 × 4 = 41.6 kg/m²
- **With 12% allowance:** **46.6 kg/m²** — at the upper bound; engineer would typically optimize spacing to target ~20–25 kg/m² by varying bar size and spacing zone by zone (column vs. middle strips).

### Case 3 — Slab-on-Grade (Warehouse, 125 mm thick)
- **Code minimum:** ρ = 0.0018 per ACI 318-19 §24.4.3.2
- **Steel area required:** As = 0.0018 × 1000 mm × 125 mm = 225 mm²/m each direction
- **Use #3 @ 250 mm c/c:** As = 284 mm²/m ✔
- **Steel weight:** (9.5²/162) × (1000/250) × 2 directions × 1.10 = **4.9 kg/m²**

---

## Common Errors

1. **Forgetting the lap splice length:** ACI 318-19 requires Class B tension splices (1.3 × ld). Omitting this adds ~8–12% to steel weight and blows the budget if ignored in quantity takeoffs.
2. **Using gross slab thickness instead of effective depth (d):** The effective depth is always *h − cover − bar_radius*. Using full thickness inflates the calculated steel area by 15–20% and masks under-design.
3. **Applying residential ratios to flat slabs:** Flat slabs concentrate shear at columns (punching shear zone). The column strip can need 2–3× more steel than the middle strip — averaging a single ratio across the whole panel grossly underestimates column zone requirements.
4. **Ignoring temperature and shrinkage steel in one-way slabs:** The transverse direction of a one-way slab still needs ρ ≥ 0.0018 (ACI 318-19 §24.4.3). Skipping this is one of the most cited errors in residential construction inspections.
5. **Confusing slab types:** A "ribbed slab" takeoff per m² of *plan area* is very different from per m² of *rib surface*. Always state the reference area when comparing kg/m² figures.

---

faq.q: What is the typical steel quantity for a residential solid slab in the US?
faq.a: For a standard residential one-way solid slab (120–180 mm thick, 5–6 m span), expect **8–15 kg/m²** of reinforcing steel. This includes bottom flexural bars, top temperature/shrinkage bars in the transverse direction, laps, chairs, and tie wire. The exact value depends on span length, live load (40 psf for residential per ASCE 7-22), and bar spacing chosen by the structural engineer.

faq.q: What does ACI 318-19 say about minimum steel ratios in slabs?
faq.a: ACI 318-19 §24.4.3.2 sets the minimum reinforcement ratio for temperature and shrinkage control at **ρ_min = 0.0018** for deformed Grade 60 bars (fy = 420 MPa). For Grade 40 or 50 bars, ρ_min = 0.0020. Structural flexural reinforcement is governed by demand but must also exceed ρ_min = 1.4/fy (§9.6.1.2). These minima exist to prevent brittle cracking, not to provide structural capacity.

faq.q: Why does a waffle slab use more steel per m² than a solid slab?
faq.a: Waffle slabs span longer distances (typically 9–15 m) and carry higher loads, requiring deeper ribs with more flexural steel in two directions plus top steel over column heads to resist negative moments. The kg/m² figure of **20–30 kg/m²** also spreads the rib steel over the gross plan area including the void pods, making it appear higher per m² even though total steel in the ribs may be similar to a thicker solid slab.

faq.q: How do I convert steel bar spacing and diameter to kg/m²?
faq.a: Use the field formula: **kg/m per layer = (diameter_mm² / 162) × (1000 / spacing_mm)**. The constant 162 comes from π/4 × 7850/1000 ≈ 0.00617, inverted and rounded. Example: 16 mm bar @ 200 mm = (256/162) × 5 = **7.9 kg/m² per layer**. Add 10–12% for laps and waste to get the procurement quantity.

faq.q: Is steel quantity the same for a post-tensioned slab?
faq.a: No — post-tensioned (PT) flat plates use **4–8 kg/m²** of passive (mild) rebar, far less than a conventionally reinforced flat slab (15–25 kg/m²). The structural work is done by high-strength PT strands (typically 12.7 mm or 15.2 mm strand, fpk = 1860 MPa) that are quoted separately in kg/m² or by strand length. Mixing PT and mild steel quantities without clarification leads to severe underestimates.

faq.q: What percentage should I add for laps, chairs, and waste?
faq.a: Industry practice in the US typically adds **10–15% to the net calculated steel weight** for procurement: ~5–8% for lap splices (per ACI 318-19 lap length requirements), ~1–2% for tie wire, ~1% for bar chairs, and ~2–3% for cutting waste. For complex slabs with many penetrations or re-entrant corners, use 15%. Government and DOT projects often specify 10% max allowance in contract documents.

faq.q: Does slab thickness directly control the steel kg/m²?
faq.a: Thickness is a primary driver but not the only one. A thicker slab has a larger effective depth d, which reduces the required steel area for the same moment (As = M / (fy × 0.9 × d)), partially offsetting the added concrete weight. However, thicker slabs also carry more self-weight, increasing moment demand. In practice, doubling slab thickness from 150 mm to 300 mm typically increases steel by only **30–60%**, not 100%, because the increased d provides more leverage.

faq.q: What is a realistic steel kg/m² for a ground-bearing slab-on-grade?
faq.a: Per **ACI 360R-10** (Guide to Design and Construction of Concrete Floors), a lightly reinforced slab-on-grade (shrinkage control only) carries ρ = 0.0018, yielding roughly **4–7 kg/m²** for a 100–150 mm slab. Heavily loaded industrial floors with subgrade drag forces or joint-free designs may use fiber reinforcement or welded wire mesh (WWM) in equivalent quantities. Structural slabs-on-grade that must transfer loads (e.g., mat foundations) can reach 20–40 kg/m².

sources.name: ACI 318-19: Building Code Requirements for Structural Concrete — American Concrete Institute
ACI 360R-10: Guide to Design and Construction of Concrete Floors — American Concrete Institute
PCI Design Handbook: Precast and Prestressed Concrete, 8th Edition — Precast/Prestressed Concrete Institute
ASCE 7-22: Minimum Design Loads and Associated Criteria for Buildings — ASCE

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
